petitioner_background: The petitioner is a judoka (judo competitor) from Georgia (the country) seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A. He intends to continue his activities as a judo competitor in the United States. He claims membership in the Georgian Judo Federation and asserts he competed on the Georgian national judo team, and he also holds a USAJUDO membership.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: At the time of filing, the petitioner submitted numerous documents as well as photographs of medals and trophies. In response to the RFE, he submitted additional photographs of trophies and medals with accompanying documentation from the Georgian Judo Federation and a judo club identifying the competition and where the petitioner placed in each competition. He also submitted results statements from competitions and photographs of himself holding various awards. He additionally submitted documents relating to competitions he participated in after the petition's filing date, including a 2022 Judo Invitational and Judo Championships in 2021.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that while the record demonstrates the petitioner won several awards in judo, it does not sufficiently demonstrate national or international recognition for excellence. Besides listing placements, the record does not provide details of the awards, such as the sponsoring organizations of the events, the official entry requirements, the level of those who participated, or the degree of attention the events attract within the field. The Director had also noted many documents lacked certified English translations and some awards appeared to be from youth competitions. Documents relating to competitions after the filing date could show intent to continue working in the field but could not establish awards received, because the petitioner must establish eligibility as of the filing date under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1). The petitioner failed to carry his burden of demonstrating the level of recognition and achievement associated with the awards.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed membership in the Georgian Judo Federation and submitted multiple statements from the Federation dated between July 2021 and November 2022 addressing his black belt status, permission to compete in USAJUDO sanctioned events, the impact of COVID-19 on judo competitions, and an injury that prevented him from competing in 2021. A November 15, 2022 statement from the Federation's president stated the petitioner is a member. The statement said 'If a sportsman meets all the criteria and can decently represent his country, he undoubtedly deserves to be a member of the national team' and described the petitioner's successful career, membership on the Georgian National Judo Team, and his status as number one in his weight categories. The petitioner also submitted a USAJUDO membership card, though on appeal he clarified USAJUDO membership was intended only to show bona fide intent to continue competing, not to meet this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director. Only one document (the November 15, 2022 statement) addressed membership in the association, but it did not state the date he became a member, and the record did not include a membership card or certificate, so the AAO could not determine whether he was a member at the time of filing as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1). The statement's reference to 'criteria' was vague — it did not clarify what criteria were required or whether they applied to membership in the Federation or the national team. The statement did not demonstrate that membership requirements are comparable to the regulatory requirement of outstanding achievements, nor that admittance is determined by nationally or internationally recognized experts. The petitioner did not submit bylaws or other official documentation of membership criteria. The statement was also confusing because it focused more on membership on the Georgian national judo team than on the Federation itself; while membership on a major national team may meet this criterion, the petitioner did not document his membership on the national team nor show the team required outstanding achievements, and the AAO will not presume every national 'team' is sufficiently exclusive.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner initially submitted Wikipedia content, social media screenshots, photos without attribution, and internet excerpts without URLs. He also submitted numerous news articles, apparently from Lelo, a Georgian newspaper, with English translations. The translations contained a signature and an illegible seal in a foreign language at the bottom of each page but did not include a certification from the translator stating the translation was complete and accurate and that the translator was competent to translate from the foreign language into English.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concurred with the Director. The Wikipedia content, social media screenshots, photos, and internet excerpts were insufficient because many omitted the title, date, and author, and no evidence showed they were published in professional or major trade publications or other major media. Regarding the news articles, although they were accompanied by English translations, the translations lacked the certification required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) that the translation is complete and accurate and that the translator is competent. Despite being informed of this deficiency in the RFE, the petitioner did not provide compliant translations. Even considering the materials, the submissions appeared to be from Lelo, a Georgian newspaper, and no evidence showed this publication qualifies as a professional or major trade publication or other major media. Additionally, many articles did not identify an author, failing the plain language requirement of the criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to satisfy at least three of the ten initial evidentiary criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3), failing all three criteria he claimed (awards, membership, and published material). Because this failure was dispositive, the AAO reserved the issue of whether the petitioner sought to enter the U.S. to continue work in his area of extraordinary ability. Although not required to conduct a final merits determination, the AAO noted it reviewed the record in the aggregate and determined it did not support a finding of sustained national or international acclaim or a 'career of acclaimed work.' The AAO emphasized that EB-1A is a highly restrictive classification for individuals already at the top of their field, not those progressing toward the top, and that even athletes performing at the major league level do not automatically meet the extraordinary ability standard.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue regarding translations: the petitioner submitted foreign-language documents (news articles and other materials) with English translations that lacked the certification required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) stating the translation is complete and accurate and that the translator is competent. Despite being notified of this deficiency in the RFE, the petitioner did not provide compliant certified translations. Additionally, the petitioner submitted evidence of competitions occurring after the filing date, which cannot be used to establish eligibility that must be shown as of the filing date under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1). No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
